nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Viejita Dwarf Cichlid inhabits shallow, slow-moving waters along the shorelines of the Colombian Llanos — the vast tropical savannah that drains into the upper Meta River. These are clear-water streams and tributaries shaded by dense marginal vegetation, with beds of leaf litter over sandy or silty substrate. The water is extremely soft and acidic, with conductivity often below 50 µS/cm.

decorPreferences: spawning caves (coconut shell halves, small clay pots, ceramic tubes)
dense planting with Cryptocoryne, Java moss, Amazon swords
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and roots for territorial division and cover

diet.notes: The Viejita Dwarf Cichlid is carnivorous, feeding on small benthic (bottom-dwelling) invertebrates in the wild. In the aquarium, live and frozen foods should form the staple of the diet: bloodworms (the larvae of chironomid midges), brine shrimp (Artemia), Daphnia, Moina, and mosquito larvae are all accepted. High-quality micro-pellets and sinking wafers are generally taken as well, but live and frozen foods are considered essential for bringing fish into breeding condition and maintaining vivid coloration. Small portions offered two or three times daily are preferable to single large feedings.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, cardinal tetras, pencilfish)
corydoras catfish (bottom zone overlap minimal in large tanks)
dither fish in upper/mid water column
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates (predation risk)
large or fast-moving fish that cause stress

summary: The Viejita Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma viejita) is a rare Colombian species seldom seen in true form — most fish sold under this name are actually A. macmasteri. Best kept in soft, acidic water as a harem of one male and two or three females.
careNotes:
The Viejita Dwarf Cichlid is a member of the macmasteri-group and originates from the Colombian Llanos, the vast tropical savannah draining into the upper Meta River basin of the Orinoco system. Water at type-collection sites is extremely soft (conductivity below 50 µS/cm) and warm (around 28°C), and the aquarium should aim to replicate these conditions: soft water (gH 1–8, kH 0–4), pH 5.5–6.8, and temperature 24–28°C. Using RO water blended with a small remineraliser, peat filtration, or blackwater extracts such as Indian almond leaves and driftwood are all practical ways to reach the target parameters; these conditioning techniques also tend to encourage spawning behaviour.

The minimum tank footprint for a single pair is 60 × 30 cm (around 60 litres). A harem arrangement of one male and 2–3 females calls for 80 × 40 cm or larger, giving the male room to hold a central territory while each female stakes out her own cave. Provide multiple small caves — coconut shell halves, unglazed ceramic pots, and ceramic tubes are all commonly used — alongside dense planting, driftwood, and a layer of dried leaf litter over a dark or fine sandy substrate. Filtration should be gentle: a sponge filter or a low-turnover hang-on unit is preferred, as this species tends to find strong currents stressful. A tightly fitting lid is advisable, since these fish are capable of jumping when startled.

The species is haremic in social structure: one dominant male may pair with and loosely defend a group of 2–3 females. Males are significantly larger (up to around 7 cm) and more colourful, displaying iridescent blue-green flanks, extended finnage with orange or red margins, and a distinctive dark upper edge to the dorsal fin. Females are smaller and yellow-gold in base colour, deepening to bright canary yellow with intensified black markings when guarding eggs or fry.

Trade confusion: the vast majority of fish sold as Apistogramma viejita in aquarium shops are actually Apistogramma macmasteri [fw-fish-141] or domestic colour strains bred from it — including the ‘Red Face,’ ‘Red Mask,’ and ‘Red Shoulder’ trade variants. True A. viejita is almost never produced commercially and typically must be sourced from specialist hobbyist breeders. For identification, true A. viejita males carry a dark top edge to the dorsal fin that is absent in A. macmasteri, and their caudal-peduncle spot is rectangular or trapezoidal in outline rather than the D-shaped, typically larger spot seen on A. macmasteri. True A. viejita also tends to show more red coloration on the head and has shorter caudal-fin extensions. Anyone seriously seeking this species is advised to obtain fish from known specialist sources and to request confirmation of species identity.
breedingNotes: The Viejita Dwarf Cichlid is a substrate cave-spawner. Conditioning the prospective pair on a varied diet of live and frozen foods for one to two weeks before introducing them improves spawning success. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5) combined with a slight temperature rise to 27–29°C generally triggers spawning. The female selects a cave and deposits 40–100 eggs on its ceiling or walls; from that point she assumes exclusive broodcare, tending the eggs closely while the male defends the surrounding territory. Eggs typically hatch within 36–72 hours, with water temperature influencing timing; the fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days. The female continues to shepherd the fry school for two to three weeks. First foods should be very fine: infusoria (microscopic aquatic organisms), micro-worms, or freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii are all suitable. If the tank is small, separating the male once the female begins tight broodcare is advisable; in larger setups the female is generally capable of driving him away from the fry zone on her own. Overall breeding difficulty is moderate: achieving the necessary soft, acidic water and conditioning the fish properly are the main prerequisites, but once those conditions are in place the species tends to spawn without much additional intervention.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma viejita
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Yucao tributary, Meta state, Colombia; Puerto Gaitán stock), habitat (shallow slow-moving shoreline waters, Llanos savannah, clear water with conductivity below 50 μS/cm, temperature 28°C), tank minimum (60×30 cm base), diet (Artemia, Daphnia, Moina, bloodworm; dried pellets accepted), breeding (cave spawner, eggs hatch 36–72 h, female exclusive broodcare), compatibility (small characins, rasboras, Otocinclus), and key identification notes distinguishing true A. viejita from A. macmasteri (dark dorsal-fin edge, rectangular/trapezoid caudal spot vs. D-shaped spot in macmasteri; true viejita not commercially produced).

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males listed as 3.0 cm SL, females 4.6 cm SL — note FishBase data appears to have sex labels inverted; males are typically larger in Apistogramma; males can reach ~7 cm TL in the trade), temperature range (23–30°C), pH lower bound (5.5), native range (upper Meta River basin, Orinoco system, Colombia), taxonomy (Cichliformes, Cichlidae, Geophaginae), trophic level 3.3 (carnivore-leaning), fecundity up to 100 eggs, IUCN Least Concern. Used 23–30°C temperature range.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma guide used for trade-standard care parameters: temperature 27–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 4–8 dGH, minimum 20-gallon tank for community setup; diet (bloodworms, tubifex, blackworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, sinking pellets); breeding (cave spawner, sponge filter, fry foods: infusoria, vinegar eels, micro-worms, then baby brine shrimp); compatibility (tetras, pencilfish, hatchetfish; avoid bettas and dwarf shrimp). No viejita-specific page; data applied as genus-level baseline.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cichlidae, described Kullander 1979), distribution (Meta River and Orinoco system, Colombia), haremic social structure (one male with multiple females), fecundity (up to 100 eggs deposited on cave ceiling), and trade confusion: A. macmasteri is frequently mislabelled as A. viejita in the aquarium trade; true A. viejita is rarely bred commercially, and most 'viejita color forms' sold are domestic A. macmasteri strains.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Provided water parameters (temperature 25–28°C, pH 5.5–6.5, hardness 2–10°dH), adult size (6–8 cm per trade measurements for males), minimum tank 60 L, diet (brine shrimp, bloodworms, daphnia, mosquito larvae, blanched vegetables; 2–3 feedings/day), breeding (female guards ~40 eggs; hatch 3–5 days; free-swimming after 5–7 days), harem structure (1 male, 3–4 females), lifespan 2–3 years (up to 4 years optimal). Adult size range 3.5–7.0 cm adopted to span wild females (small) through trade males (larger).

fish.breedingNotes: The Viejita Dwarf Cichlid is a cave spawner. The female deposits 40–100 eggs on the ceiling of the chosen cave and provides exclusive broodcare from that point. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5) and conditioning on live or frozen foods are considered essential prerequisites. Eggs hatch in 36–72 hours; the fry are generally free-swimming within a week and can be started on infusoria or micro-worms, transitioning to brine shrimp nauplii (Artemia) as they grow.
